serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Thai basil pork (meat only) is approximately 150 g, representing the pork and herb component without rice.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Basil Pork (Meat Only) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Thai basil pork (meat only), CR ID F06100052. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil pork (meat only) in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at approximately 35 based on the analogous category of mixed meat-and-vegetable stir-fry dishes with low carbohydrate content (4.88 g/100g). The dominant carbohydrate contributors are oyster sauce and residual sugars from fish sauce; these are low-GI condiments. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 4.88 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 2.6 g, confirming very low GL. This estimate is conservative and defensible given the low carbohydrate density of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 573 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 860 mg sodium, representing 37–57% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced sauce quantities when ordering, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal to keep total daily sodium within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 4.88 g per 100 g and sugar 3.58 g per 100 g; high protein (12.97 g/100 g) supports glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai basil pork (meat only) is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as the protein component of a meal; pair with low-GI vegetables rather than large portions of white rice to maintain postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 10.9 g per 100 g from pork; sodium 573 mg per 100 g from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce; saturated fat breakdown not available from HPB source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to 100 g, request reduced sauce to lower sodium intake, and consider substituting lean pork loin for fattier cuts to reduce saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies inflammatory pathways; sodium 573 mg per 100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting pork with chicken breast, tofu, or tempeh to reduce arachidonic acid load, and monitor personal tolerance to chilli (nightshade) which may trigger flares in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Basil Pork (Meat Only), known in Thai as หมูผัดกะเพรา, is a popular Thai stir-fried dish consisting of minced or sliced pork cooked with fresh Thai holy basil (kaphrao), bird's eye chillies, garlic, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. This record captures the meat-only component, excluding the customary fried egg and steamed rice accompaniments. The dish is characterised by a bold, savoury, and mildly spicy flavour profile with aromatic herbal notes from Thai holy basil. The texture is tender and slightly saucy. Per 100 g, the dish provides 169 kcal, 12.97 g protein, 10.9 g fat, and 4.88 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 573 mg per 100 g due to the combination of fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, which is a clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate content (GI estimated ~35, GL ~2.6 per 150 g serving) makes it suitable as a protein component for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Calcium is 23 mg and iron is 1.48 mg per 100 g. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, shellfish, soy, or gluten allergies. It is widely consumed in Thailand and available across Southeast Asian food courts and hawker centres.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F06100052) at confidence 0.95. Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature given the absence of a direct GI measurement for this specific dish; this is the only element reducing certainty below absolute, but the estimate is well-supported by the low carbohydrate density of the dish.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Thai basil pork preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06100052) as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact match for this dish to supply these values. GI is an analogous estimate (~35) based on the low-carbohydrate mixed meat stir-fry category from Atkinson et al. (2021), American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; no direct GI measurement for Thai basil pork exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. The dish is not halal-certified as standard preparations use pork. Dietary tags 'high_protein' and 'keto' from the input record are preserved as contextually appropriate given the macronutrient profile.
